Category: --- COST & WORK CLASSIFICATIONS: standards that organize construction costs and specifications ---

Description: The 50-division classification system published by CSI/CSC that organizes construction costs and specifications by type of work. The single most important standard for cross-company financial data comparison. Divisions map directly to building systems: Division 03 = Concrete → Foundations + Structural Frame, Division 26 = Electrical → Electrical system. Used for cost codes, specification sections, and bid packages. Three levels: division (2-digit), section (4-digit), subsection (6-digit). The UCDM's primary Rosetta Stone between company-specific codes and universal categories.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 2018 (50 divisions); CSI Practice Guide; maintained by Construction Specifications Institute
Common Values: Div 02 Existing Conditions, 03 Concrete, 05 Metals, 07 Thermal/Moisture, 08 Openings, 09 Finishes, 21 Fire Suppression, 22 Plumbing, 23 HVAC, 26 Electrical, 27 Communications, 31 Earthwork, 32 Exterior Improvements, 33 Utilities
Source Systems: Procore (cost_codes); Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; Foundation; Spectrum; estimating software (Sage Estimating, WinEst, ProEst)
Procore Coverage: High — cost_codes and cost_code_lists tables map to MasterFormat divisions. standard_cost_code field provides industry-standard mapping. Foundational to all financial tracking.
Benchmarking Use: Normalize company cost codes to MasterFormat divisions for cross-company cost comparison by building system
Notes: Cost codes vary wildly between companies — some follow MasterFormat strictly, others use proprietary numbering. The first 2 digits of a MasterFormat code reliably map to building systems. Company code → MasterFormat division → building system is the primary financial-to-physical mapping path.

Description: A numbered section within a project's technical specifications organized by MasterFormat — defines quality standards, materials, methods, and acceptance criteria for a specific scope of work. Specification sections are the contractual quality standard against which work is inspected and accepted. Each section maps to a MasterFormat number (e.g., 03 30 00 Cast-in-Place Concrete, 09 29 00 Gypsum Board). Sections govern submittals (what must be submitted for approval) and inspections (what must pass).
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat (section numbering); CSI SectionFormat (internal organization: General, Products, Execution); AIA MasterSpec; ARCOM SpecText
Common Values: Part 1 General (scope, references, submittals), Part 2 Products (materials, manufacturers, standards), Part 3 Execution (installation, quality control, protection)
Source Systems: Procore (specification_sections); Bluebeam; ProjectWise; Newforma; Specpoint; physical spec books
Procore Coverage: Medium — specification_sections table exists (section_number, description, revision) and is READY, but adoption depends on whether project teams enter spec sections into Procore. Many projects keep specs as PDF uploads only.
Benchmarking Use: Compare specification section coverage (% of project specs entered as structured data) across projects
Notes: Specification sections link to submittals (specification_section_id on submittals table — sparse but high-value) and to RFIs (spec reference in question). The spec section number directly maps to MasterFormat and building systems.

Description: A company-standard or industry-standard template of cost codes used to set up new projects — ensures consistency across an organization's project portfolio. Companies may maintain multiple lists for different project types. Standard lists enable cross-project comparison within a company. The UCDM maps company lists to MasterFormat for cross-company comparison.
Industry Standard: Company-specific; some industry templates (AGC, AIA); MasterFormat-aligned
Common Values: 3-level hierarchies (division.section.subsection), flat code lists, project-type-specific lists; 50–500 codes per list depending on granularity
Source Systems: Procore (cost_code_lists); Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; Foundation; Excel
Procore Coverage: High — cost_code_lists table tracks company cost code hierarchies. READY.
Benchmarking Use: Compare cost code list granularity and standardization across companies to assess data quality readiness
Notes: Standard cost code lists are the prerequisite for cross-project financial comparison. Companies without standard lists have inconsistent cost codes across projects — making benchmarking unreliable. cost_code_lists table enables assessment of code standardization.

Description: Work Breakdown Structure code templates that provide a second dimension of cost classification beyond cost codes — segments costs by phase, location, category, or other company-defined dimension. WBS codes add granularity within a cost code: cost code 03 30 00 (Concrete) + WBS 'Foundation' vs. WBS 'Elevated Slab'. Key WBS values like 'Rework' and 'Warranty' are signals for rework cost detection. Adoption varies widely.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK WBS (§5.4); MIL-STD-881 (defense WBS); company-specific WBS structures
Common Values: Phase-based (Design, Construction, Closeout), Location-based (Building A, Level 3), Category-based (Rework, Warranty, Original Scope), Hybrid
Source Systems: Procore (wbs_codes); Primavera P6; MS Project; CMiC; Viewpoint
Procore Coverage: Medium — wbs_codes table exists and is READY, but WBS adoption varies widely across companies. Some use extensively, others not at all.
Benchmarking Use: WBS-level cost segmentation enables building-system-specific and location-specific benchmarking
Notes: WBS 'Rework' and 'Warranty' segments are high-confidence signals for rework cost detection (validated in insight #1). Budget codes serve a similar reporting purpose but are less standardized.

Description: Company-specific grouping codes applied to budget line items for executive reporting — distinct from cost codes (transactional) and WBS codes (segmentation). Budget codes group budget lines into roll-up categories (Structure, Enclosure, MEP, General Conditions, Contingency). Less standardized than cost codes but valuable for executive dashboards and portfolio views.
Industry Standard: Company-specific; loosely aligned with UniFormat categories; no universal standard
Common Values: Structure, Enclosure, MEP, General Conditions, Contingency, Site, Interiors, Overhead, Fee
Source Systems: Procore (budget_codes); Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — budget_codes table exists but NOT READY for import. Not all companies use budget codes.
Benchmarking Use: Compare budget code roll-up categories across companies to enable executive-level cost comparison
Notes: Budget codes with values like 'Rework' or 'Warranty' are rework cost detection signals (339 cos validated). Budget codes are the least standardized of the three code types (cost code > WBS > budget code). Can map to building system groups.

Category: --- BUILDING & ELEMENT CLASSIFICATIONS: standards that classify what is being built ---

Description: A building element classification system that organizes construction by functional systems rather than by product type — the complement to MasterFormat. While MasterFormat classifies by WHAT is installed (materials, products), UniFormat classifies by WHAT IT DOES (function, system). Used for conceptual estimating, systems-level cost analysis, and facility lifecycle management. Levels: Level 1 (Major Group Elements: A Substructure, B Shell, C Interiors, D Services), Level 2, Level 3.
Industry Standard: ASTM E1557 (UniFormat); maintained by CSI/CSC alongside MasterFormat
Common Values: A Substructure (A10 Foundations, A20 Basement), B Shell (B10 Superstructure, B20 Exterior Enclosure, B30 Roofing), C Interiors (C10 Construction, C20 Stairs, C30 Finishes), D Services (D10 Conveying, D20 Plumbing, D30 HVAC, D40 Fire Protection, D50 Electrical)
Source Systems: Estimating software (RSMeans, DProfiler, Sage Estimating); BIM tools (Revit); Procore (conceptual — not a native classification)
Procore Coverage: Low — Procore does not natively use UniFormat. No dedicated UniFormat table. Budget codes may loosely align with UniFormat categories but are not standardized.
Benchmarking Use: Compare building system costs at UniFormat Level 2 across project types for conceptual benchmarking
Notes: UniFormat is the natural classification for the UCDM Building Elements domain. The taxonomy-building-elements.csv categories (Foundations, Structural Frame, Exterior Enclosure, etc.) align with UniFormat Level 2 groups. Cross-reference: MasterFormat divisions map to UniFormat elements.

Description: A comprehensive classification system covering ALL construction entities — buildings, spaces, elements, products, phases, disciplines, and more. Organized in 15 tables covering different facets of construction (Table 21: Elements, Table 22: Work Results, Table 23: Products, Table 31: Phases, Table 33: Disciplines, Table 34: Organizational Roles). Designed as a unifying framework for BIM and facility management. Rarely used directly in field operations but important for data interoperability.
Industry Standard: ASTM E2271 (OmniClass); maintained by CSI; aligns with ISO 12006-2 (building classification)
Common Values: Table 11 Construction Entities, Table 13 Spaces, Table 21 Elements (UniFormat-based), Table 22 Work Results (MasterFormat-based), Table 23 Products, Table 31 Phases, Table 33 Disciplines, Table 34 Organizational Roles, Table 36 Information
Source Systems: BIM authoring tools (Revit, ArchiCAD); IFC-based platforms; asset management systems; COBie
Procore Coverage: None — Procore does not use OmniClass natively. No OmniClass tables or fields in the data model.
Benchmarking Use: OmniClass Table 21 (Elements) provides a universal benchmarking framework for building system costs
Notes: OmniClass is the most comprehensive classification but the least adopted in practice. Its value is as a mapping target — the UCDM can map its own domains to OmniClass tables for interoperability with BIM and FM systems.

Description: Industry Foundation Classes — an open data standard for BIM that defines entity types, properties, and relationships for all building elements. IFC entities (IfcWall, IfcDoor, IfcDuctSegment, etc.) provide a machine-readable classification of building elements that complements UniFormat and MasterFormat. Used for BIM coordination, clash detection, and digital twin integration.
Industry Standard: ISO 16739 (IFC); buildingSMART International; IFC4 (current), IFC4x3 (infrastructure)
Common Values: IfcWall, IfcSlab, IfcBeam, IfcColumn, IfcDoor, IfcWindow, IfcDuctSegment, IfcPipeSegment, IfcCableSegment, IfcFireSuppressionTerminal
Source Systems: Revit; ArchiCAD; Tekla; Navisworks; Solibri; BIM 360; open BIM platforms
Procore Coverage: None — Procore does not natively import or classify by IFC entity type. BIM models are viewed in Procore but IFC classification is not exposed as structured data.
Benchmarking Use: IFC entity type mapping enables BIM-to-UCDM building element cross-reference
Notes: IFC is the bridge between BIM and construction management data. Mapping IFC entities to UCDM building elements enables automated classification of model elements into the taxonomy. The coordination_issue table in Procore captures BIM coordination findings.

Category: --- SAFETY & HAZARD CLASSIFICATIONS: standards that categorize safety risks and regulatory requirements ---

Description: Standardized safety hazard types defined by OSHA for construction — the Focus Four hazards (Fall, Struck By, Electrical, Caught In/Between) plus additional categories. These categories align directly with observation.hazard_name in Procore — an OSHA-aligned structured taxonomy with 16 types having 50+ companies each. The primary classification for safety observation analysis and recurrence tracking.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926 (Construction Safety Standards); OSHA Focus Four Hazards; BLS injury classification
Common Values: Fall Protection, Struck By, Electrical, Caught In/Between, Housekeeping, PPE, Scaffolding, Excavation, Ladder Safety, Fire Prevention, Confined Space, Crane/Rigging, Trenching, Welding/Hot Work
Source Systems: Procore (observation.hazard_name); iAuditor/SafetyCulture; OSHA reporting systems; company safety databases
Procore Coverage: High — observation.hazard_name provides OSHA-aligned structured taxonomy. 55% populated on safety observations. 16 types with 50+ cos each.
Benchmarking Use: Compare hazard type frequency and recurrence rates across project types and trades to benchmark safety risk profiles
Notes: OSHA hazard categories are validated as the primary safety classification in the UCDM (insight #11). hazard_name is 45% null — enrichment opportunity. Recurrence rates range 29–45% by hazard type. Fall Protection has highest volume across all project types.

Description: The OSHA system for classifying workplace injuries and illnesses by severity — determines which incidents are 'recordable' and drive TRIR and DART rate calculations. Recordable = medical treatment beyond first aid, restricted duty, days away from work, or fatality. The classification directly determines a company's safety metrics for prequalification.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1904 (Recording and Reporting); OSHA Forms 300, 300A, 301; BLS Survey of Occupational Injuries and Illnesses
Common Values: First Aid Only (not recordable), Medical Treatment, Restricted Duty, Days Away, Transfer, Fatality; OSHA 300 Log categories
Source Systems: Procore (incident, incident_record); OSHA electronic reporting; company safety databases; EMR calculation tools
Procore Coverage: Medium — incident and incident_record tables capture severity details, but OSHA recordability classification may require manual determination from incident_record fields.
Benchmarking Use: Compare TRIR and DART rates computed from incident classification across companies and project types
Notes: OSHA recordkeeping classification is the foundation for TRIR and DART metrics. Accurate classification requires trained safety professionals. Under-classification (recording a recordable as first-aid) distorts safety metrics.

Description: The ANSI/BLS system for classifying injuries by nature (laceration, fracture, sprain), body part (hand, back, knee), and cause (fall, struck by, overexertion) — provides detailed injury taxonomy for pattern analysis. More granular than OSHA recordkeeping which only classifies severity. Used for root cause analysis and targeted prevention programs.
Industry Standard: ANSI Z16.1 (Injury Classification); BLS Occupational Injury and Illness Classification System (OIICS)
Common Values: Nature: laceration, contusion, fracture, sprain/strain, burn. Body part: hand/finger, back, knee, head, eye. Cause: fall, struck by, overexertion, caught in, contact with
Source Systems: Procore (incident_record body part and nature fields); safety management platforms; workers comp systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — incident_record table captures injury details but nature/body part/cause fields depend on data entry quality and consistency.
Benchmarking Use: Compare injury nature and body part distributions across trades to identify trade-specific injury prevention priorities
Notes: Injury classification enables trade-specific prevention: fall-related hand/wrist injuries in steel erection, back injuries in concrete, eye injuries in welding. Cross-reference with OSHA hazard categories for complete safety risk profiling.

Category: --- BUILDING CODES & REGULATORY STANDARDS: codes that govern what and how to build ---

Description: The model building code adopted (with local amendments) by most US jurisdictions — governs structural design, fire safety, egress, accessibility, and building system requirements. IBC classification determines construction type (Type I–V), occupancy group (A, B, E, H, I, M, R, S, U), and fire rating requirements. These classifications directly affect project cost, schedule, and building system requirements. Updated on a 3-year cycle.
Industry Standard: ICC International Building Code (IBC 2021, 2024); adopted with amendments by state/local jurisdictions
Common Values: Construction Type: I-A, I-B, II-A, II-B, III-A, III-B, IV-A through IV-C, V-A, V-B. Occupancy: Assembly (A), Business (B), Educational (E), Factory (F), High Hazard (H), Institutional (I), Mercantile (M), Residential (R), Storage (S), Utility (U)
Source Systems: Building code analysis software; Procore (as project attributes — construction type, occupancy); permit tracking systems
Procore Coverage: Low — IBC classifications tracked as project attributes or custom fields, not as structured code data. Construction type and occupancy are project-level attributes, not dedicated tables.
Benchmarking Use: Compare project cost and schedule by IBC construction type and occupancy to benchmark code-driven cost impacts
Notes: IBC construction type directly affects cost — Type I-A (most fire resistant) costs significantly more than Type V-B (least). Occupancy group affects egress, sprinkler, and fire alarm requirements. Cross-reference with Building Elements domain for system-level code requirements.

Description: National Fire Protection Association standards governing fire protection, life safety, and electrical systems — the technical basis for fire code requirements. NFPA 13 (sprinklers), NFPA 72 (fire alarm), NFPA 101 (Life Safety Code), and NEC/NFPA 70 (electrical) are the most impactful on construction. NFPA standards define system design criteria, installation methods, testing requirements, and inspection frequencies.
Industry Standard: NFPA 13 (Sprinkler Systems), NFPA 72 (Fire Alarm), NFPA 101 (Life Safety Code), NFPA 70/NEC (Electrical), NFPA 25 (Inspection/Testing of Water Systems)
Common Values: Sprinkler system design (ordinary/extra hazard), Fire alarm system type (addressable/conventional), Egress capacity, Electrical service capacity, Fire pump requirements
Source Systems: Procore (inspections, observations — reference NFPA in criteria); specialized fire protection design software; code analysis tools
Procore Coverage: Low — NFPA standards referenced in inspection criteria and specification sections but not structured as classification data in Procore.
Benchmarking Use: Compare fire protection system costs and inspection deficiency rates by NFPA standard requirements across project types
Notes: NFPA standards drive significant cost in fire protection building elements. NFPA 13 sprinkler requirements vary by occupancy and construction type. Fire protection inspection deficiency rates (Quality & Safety domain) can be segmented by NFPA standard.

Description: Americans with Disabilities Act Accessibility Guidelines (ADAAG) and ICC A117.1 — govern accessible design requirements for public and commercial buildings. Affects door widths, ramp slopes, restroom layouts, signage, elevator requirements, and path of travel clearances. Compliance is both a legal requirement and a project completion condition. Accessibility violations are common punch list items.
Industry Standard: ADA/ADAAG (federal); ICC A117.1 (Accessible and Usable Buildings); Fair Housing Act (residential); state accessibility codes
Common Values: Accessible route (36" min clear), Door clearance (32" min), Ramp slope (1:12 max), Accessible restroom, Elevator requirements, Signage (Braille), Parking (van accessible)
Source Systems: Code analysis software; Procore (inspections, punch items — accessibility deficiencies); accessibility consultants
Procore Coverage: Low — ADA requirements referenced in inspections and punch items but not structured as classification data. Accessibility violations appear in punch_item.name via keyword clustering.
Benchmarking Use: Compare accessibility-related punch item frequency across project types to benchmark compliance risk
Notes: Accessibility violations are a common source of punch items and rework — particularly in restrooms, door hardware, and signage. ADA compliance inspections may be third-party or by the building department.

Description: Energy conservation codes (IECC, ASHRAE 90.1) and above-code programs — govern building envelope performance, HVAC efficiency, lighting power density, and renewable energy requirements. Energy code compliance affects construction cost (insulation, glazing performance, equipment efficiency) and requires specific testing and commissioning. Increasingly stringent with each code cycle.
Industry Standard: IECC (International Energy Conservation Code); ASHRAE 90.1 (Energy Standard for Buildings); state energy codes (Title 24 in CA); stretch codes
Common Values: Climate zones 1–8, Envelope U-values, SHGC requirements, Lighting power density (LPD), HVAC efficiency (SEER, EER, COP), Air barrier testing, Commissioning requirements
Source Systems: Energy modeling software (eQUEST, EnergyPlus); Procore (as commissioning inspections); blower door testing equipment
Procore Coverage: Low — energy code compliance tracked through commissioning inspections and forms, not as structured classification data. Climate zone is a project attribute.
Benchmarking Use: Compare energy code compliance testing results across climate zones and project types
Notes: Energy code stringency varies dramatically by jurisdiction (California Title 24 vs. minimum IECC states). Code-driven system requirements affect HVAC, envelope, and lighting building element costs. Cross-reference with Building Elements and Phases (commissioning).

Category: --- SUSTAINABILITY & CERTIFICATION: green building and performance standards ---

Description: Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design — the most widely recognized green building certification system. Point-based system across categories: Sustainable Sites, Water Efficiency, Energy & Atmosphere, Materials & Resources, Indoor Environmental Quality, Innovation. Certification levels: Certified (40–49 pts), Silver (50–59), Gold (60–79), Platinum (80+). LEED requirements affect material selection, construction waste management, commissioning scope, and documentation.
Industry Standard: USGBC LEED v4.1 (BD+C, ID+C, O+M); LEED Online documentation requirements
Common Values: Certified, Silver, Gold, Platinum; credit categories: Sustainable Sites, Water, Energy, Materials, IEQ, Innovation
Source Systems: Procore (project attributes, waste tracking via daily_log_waste for MR credits); LEED Online; energy modeling software
Procore Coverage: Low — LEED certification level tracked as project attribute. Construction waste diversion (daily_log_waste) supports Materials & Resources credits. No dedicated LEED tracking table.
Benchmarking Use: Compare LEED certification level distribution and cost premium across project types and regions
Notes: LEED projects require specific documentation that maps to UCDM domains: waste diversion (Field Operations), commissioning (Quality & Safety), material submittals (Documents), energy modeling (Building Elements). LEED cost premium typically 2–8%.

Description: A performance-based standard focused on occupant health and wellness — covers air, water, nourishment, light, fitness, comfort, and mind. Less common than LEED but growing rapidly, especially in commercial office and healthcare. WELL requirements affect HVAC design (air quality), lighting design (circadian), and finishes (low-VOC materials). Requires ongoing performance testing.
Industry Standard: IWBI WELL Building Standard v2; WELL Performance Rating
Common Values: Silver, Gold, Platinum; categories: Air, Water, Nourishment, Light, Movement, Thermal Comfort, Sound, Materials, Mind, Community, Innovation
Source Systems: WELL Online; Procore (project attribute); air quality monitoring systems; commissioning tools
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in Procore. WELL certification is a project attribute managed outside construction management systems.
Benchmarking Use: Compare WELL certification adoption rates across project types and regions
Notes: WELL is gaining traction in commercial office, healthcare, and education. Requirements overlap with LEED (air quality, materials) but add occupant-focused performance testing. Cross-reference with Building Elements (HVAC, lighting, finishes).

Description: Emerging standards for climate resilience (RELi, FORTIFIED) and net-zero energy/carbon performance — increasingly required by institutional owners and government agencies. Resilience standards address flood, wind, seismic, and wildfire hazards. Net-zero standards require energy production to equal or exceed consumption. Both drive building system design decisions and construction scope.
Industry Standard: RELi 2.0 (Resilience); IBHS FORTIFIED (Wind/Hail); ILFI Living Building Challenge; Architecture 2030; local net-zero mandates
Common Values: Net Zero Energy, Net Zero Carbon, Net Zero Water; FORTIFIED Roof, Gold, Silver; Living Building Challenge Petals
Source Systems: Procore (project attributes); energy modeling; resilience assessment tools; renewable energy monitoring
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in Procore. Resilience and net-zero certifications are project attributes managed outside construction management systems.
Benchmarking Use: Compare resilience and net-zero adoption rates and cost impacts across regions and project types
Notes: Resilience requirements are jurisdiction-specific (coastal flood zones, hurricane regions, wildfire-urban interface). Net-zero mandates are expanding — several states and cities require net-zero for new government buildings.

Category: --- CONTRACT & DELIVERY STANDARDS: standards governing project delivery and contractual frameworks ---

Description: The American Institute of Architects standard contract document series — the most widely used contract forms in US construction. Key documents: A101 (Owner-Contractor, Stipulated Sum), A201 (General Conditions), A401 (Contractor-Sub), G-series (administrative forms: G701 Change Order, G702 Pay Application, G704 Certificate of Substantial Completion). AIA documents define roles, responsibilities, and workflows that directly shape how financial instruments, documents, and quality records flow.
Industry Standard: AIA A101, A201, A401, B101 (Owner-Architect), G-series (G701, G702, G703, G704, G706); updated every ~10 years
Common Values: A101 Stipulated Sum, A102 Cost Plus Fee, A133 CM at Risk, A141 Design-Build; B101 Owner-Architect; G701 Change Order, G702 Pay App, G703 Continuation Sheet, G704 Substantial Completion
Source Systems: Contract management in Procore; DocuSign; physical contracts; AIA Contract Documents Online
Procore Coverage: Low — AIA document types are not classified in Procore. Contracts uploaded as documents. Change order forms (G701), pay applications (G702/G703), and certificates (G704) are tracked functionally but not classified by AIA document number.
Benchmarking Use: Compare contract form usage (AIA vs. ConsensusDocs vs. custom) across project types and delivery methods
Notes: AIA documents define the workflows modeled in the UCDM — change order flow (G701 → Financial Instruments), pay application flow (G702/G703 → Billing & Payment), substantial completion (G704 → Phases). Understanding which AIA forms a project uses predicts which UCDM workflows will have structured data.

Description: The taxonomy of project delivery methods that determines how design, construction, and risk are allocated between parties. Each delivery method creates different document flows, change order patterns, and financial instrument relationships. Key methods: Design-Bid-Build (DBB), CM at Risk (CMAR), Design-Build (DB), Integrated Project Delivery (IPD), Multi-Prime, Construction Management Agency.
Industry Standard: AIA delivery method documents (A-series); AGC project delivery guidelines; DBIA Design-Build Institute; Lean Construction Institute (IPD)
Common Values: Design-Bid-Build (traditional), CM at Risk (GMP), Design-Build, Integrated Project Delivery (IPD), Multi-Prime, CM Agency, Progressive Design-Build
Source Systems: Procore (project attribute); contract documents; company setup
Procore Coverage: Low — delivery method tracked as project attribute or custom field. Not a structured classification in Procore. Significantly affects which financial instruments and change order workflows are active.
Benchmarking Use: Compare cost growth
Notes: schedule growth
column10: and change order rates across delivery methods to benchmark delivery strategy effectiveness
column11: Delivery method is the single most important project attribute for benchmarking — it determines contract structure, risk allocation, change order patterns, and financial instrument usage. CM at Risk (GMP) projects have contingency; Design-Bid-Build do not. IPD projects have shared savings/risk.

Description: The Association for the Advancement of Cost Engineering recommended practices — define cost estimating, scheduling, and project controls methodologies. Key standards: 18R-97 (Cost Estimate Classification), 10S-90 (Cost Engineering), EVMS practices. AACE standards provide the methodology framework for financial metrics (EAC, CPI, SPI) and cost classification (estimate classes 1–5).
Industry Standard: AACE International Recommended Practices: 18R-97 (Estimate Classification), 10S-90 (Cost Engineering), 29R-03 (Forensic Schedule Analysis), 86R-14 (Earned Value)
Common Values: Estimate Class 1 (definitive, ±5-10%), Class 2 (detailed, ±10-15%), Class 3 (budget, ±15-25%), Class 4 (feasibility, ±25-40%), Class 5 (concept, ±40-100%)
Source Systems: Procore (financial modules apply AACE methodology); Primavera; cost engineering tools; estimating software
Procore Coverage: Low — AACE methodology is applied through financial tools but not classified as structured data. Estimate classification is not a tracked field.
Benchmarking Use: Compare estimate accuracy (final cost vs. original estimate) by AACE estimate class to benchmark estimating performance
Notes: AACE estimate classes map to project phases — Class 5 at feasibility, Class 1 at IFC. Tracking how estimate accuracy improves through phases benchmarks the estimating process. Cross-reference with Financial Instruments (budget variance, EAC) and Phases.

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: KPIs derived from standards adoption and classification quality ---

Description: Percentage of a company's projects using a consistent standard cost code list — measures data readiness for cross-project benchmarking. Formula: Projects with Standard Cost Codes / Total Projects × 100. Companies with < 50% standardization have unreliable cross-project financial data. Cost code standardization is the prerequisite for all financial benchmarking.
Industry Standard: Company-specific quality target
Common Values: Target: > 80% standardization; < 50% indicates significant data quality risk
Source Systems: Derived from: cost_codes, cost_code_lists, projects
Procore Coverage: High — computed from cost_codes and cost_code_lists tables
Benchmarking Use: Compare cost code standardization rates across companies to assess benchmarking readiness
Notes: Cost code standardization is the #1 data quality factor for financial benchmarking. Companies with standardized codes can compare costs across projects; those without cannot. The UCDM must normalize non-standard codes to MasterFormat.

Description: Percentage of a company's cost codes that map to recognized MasterFormat divisions — measures the degree to which company codes can be normalized for cross-company comparison. Formula: Cost Codes with Valid MasterFormat Mapping / Total Cost Codes × 100. Some codes (General Conditions, Overhead, Fee) don't map to MasterFormat building systems.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat mapping conventions
Common Values: Target: > 70% of cost codes mappable to MasterFormat divisions; remainder typically General Conditions, Overhead, Fee
Source Systems: Derived from: cost_codes (code, name, standard_cost_code)
Procore Coverage: Medium — standard_cost_code field on cost_codes provides some mapping, but completeness varies
Benchmarking Use: Compare MasterFormat mapping coverage across companies to identify normalization gaps
Notes: MasterFormat mapping is the bridge from company-specific financial data to UCDM building system cost comparison. standard_cost_code field provides the mapping when populated. NLP on cost code names supplements for unmapped codes.

Description: Percentage of project specification sections entered as structured data vs. remaining as PDF uploads — measures the quality of specification cross-referencing for submittals and RFIs. Formula: Structured Spec Sections / Estimated Total Sections × 100.
Industry Standard: CSI section count norms by project type
Common Values: Typical project: 50–200 spec sections; coverage varies widely
Source Systems: Derived from: specification_sections (count per project)
Procore Coverage: Medium — specification_sections table exists but adoption varies widely by project
Benchmarking Use: Compare specification section data entry rates across projects to assess documentation quality
Notes: Specification section coverage directly affects the quality of submittal-to-spec and RFI-to-spec cross-referencing. Projects with 0 structured sections lose all spec-based analytics.

Description: Percentage of safety observations with a structured hazard_name classification — measures the quality of safety data for OSHA hazard analysis. Formula: Observations with hazard_name / Total Safety Observations × 100. Currently 55% populated across the portfolio — a significant enrichment opportunity.
Industry Standard: OSHA hazard category standards
Common Values: Current: 55% populated; target: > 80% for reliable hazard analysis
Source Systems: Derived from: observation (category = 'Safety', hazard_name null rate)
Procore Coverage: High — computed from observation.hazard_name null rate
Benchmarking Use: Compare safety classification completeness across companies to identify data quality improvement opportunities
Notes: Safety classification completeness directly affects the reliability of hazard recurrence analysis (insight #11). Companies with > 80% hazard_name population have reliable safety risk profiles; those with < 30% do not.

Description: Percentage of projects or companies using WBS codes for cost segmentation — measures the depth of financial data granularity available for building-element-level benchmarking. Formula: Projects with WBS Codes / Total Financial Projects × 100.
Industry Standard: PMI WBS methodology adoption
Common Values: Varies: some companies use extensively (5+ segments per cost code), others not at all
Source Systems: Derived from: wbs_codes (count per project), budget_line_items (wbs_code_id population rate)
Procore Coverage: Medium — computed from wbs_codes table presence per project
Benchmarking Use: Compare WBS adoption rates across companies to assess granularity of financial data
Notes: WBS adoption is the key differentiator for deep financial benchmarking. Companies with WBS codes can track costs at the building-element level within a cost code (e.g., rework vs. original scope). Without WBS, only cost-code-level granularity is available.

Description: Percentage of projects pursuing third-party certification (LEED, WELL, FORTIFIED, etc.) — measures the market penetration of green building and performance standards. Formula: Certified or Pursuing Projects / Total Projects × 100. Can be segmented by certification type, level, and project type.
Industry Standard: USGBC adoption reports; IWBI market data
Common Values: LEED: ~30% of new commercial; WELL: ~5% and growing; varies widely by region and owner type
Source Systems: Derived from: projects (certification attributes or custom fields)
Procore Coverage: Low — certification tracked as project attributes or custom fields, not structured classification data
Benchmarking Use: Compare certification adoption rates across regions and project types to benchmark sustainability trends
Notes: Certification adoption is driven by owner requirements — government and institutional owners have higher LEED rates than private developers. Cross-reference with project cost to assess certification cost premium.
